intro: Charts and diagrams display data visually. Knowing how to read, interpret and draw them accurately is fundamental to the statistics section of your GCSE examination.
explanation:
Bar charts and pie charts:
- Bar chart: height (or length) of each bar represents frequency. Bars have equal width and equal gaps.
- Pie chart: each sector's angle represents its proportion. Use: angle = (frequency / total) x 360.

Frequency polygons:
- Plot the midpoint of each class interval against frequency, then join the points with straight lines.
- Used for continuous grouped data.

Scatter graphs and correlation:
- Plot one variable on x-axis and another on y-axis.
- Correlation describes the relationship:
  - Positive correlation: as x increases, y increases.
  - Negative correlation: as x increases, y decreases.
  - No correlation: no clear pattern.
- Line of best fit: a straight line through the middle of the scatter of points. Draw it so roughly equal numbers of points are above and below.
- Use the line of best fit to estimate values: interpolation (within the data range) is reliable; extrapolation (outside the data range) is unreliable.

Stem-and-leaf diagrams:
- Data is split into stems (tens) and leaves (units).
- Back-to-back stem-and-leaf diagrams compare two data sets.
- Always write leaves in order and include a key.

Two-way tables:
- Show data for two categories simultaneously.
- Fill in any missing values using row and column totals.

worked_example.question: A survey finds: 15 students prefer sport, 9 prefer art, 6 prefer music, and 30 prefer drama. Calculate the angle for each sector in a pie chart.
worked_example.final_answer: Sport: 90 degrees, Art: 54 degrees, Music: 36 degrees, Drama: 180 degrees

common_mistakes: Plotting bar charts with unequal bar widths — all bars in a standard bar chart must have the same width.
Drawing a line of best fit that only passes through the first and last point — it should reflect the overall trend with similar numbers of points on each side.
Forgetting to include a key on a stem-and-leaf diagram — examiners require a key like '5 | 3 means 53'.
